   **[Presidential system](/wiki/Presidential_system "Presidential system"):** Head of government (president) is popularly elected and independent of the legislature.

  Presidential republic

   **Hybrid systems:**

  [Semi-presidential republic](/wiki/Semi-presidential_republic "Semi-presidential republic"): Executive president is independent of the legislature; head of government is appointed by the president and is accountable to the legislature.

  [Assembly-independent republic](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_system_of_government#Assembly-independent_republics "List of countries by system of government"): Head of government (president or directory) is elected by the legislature, but is not accountable to it.

  [Theocratic](/wiki/Theocracy "Theocracy") republic: Supreme leader is head of both the state and the faith, and holds significant executive and legislative power

  [Semi-constitutional monarchy](#Executive_monarchy_versus_ceremonial_monarchy): Monarch holds significant executive or legislative power but is still restricted by the constitution.

  [Absolute monarchy](/wiki/Absolute_monarchy "Absolute monarchy"): Monarch has unlimited power.

  [One-party state](/wiki/One-party_state "One-party state"): Power is constitutionally linked to a single political party.

**Constitutional monarchy**, also known as **limited monarchy**, **parliamentary monarchy** or **democratic monarchy**, is a form of [monarchy](/wiki/Monarchy "Monarchy") in which the monarch exercises their authority in accordance with a constitution and is not alone in making decisions.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlumCameronBarnes19702Nnk67–268-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKurian2011[[Category:Wikipedia_articles_needing_page_number_citations_from_December_2015]]<sup_class="noprint_Inline-Template_"_style="white-space:nowrap;">[<i>[[Wikipedia:Citing_sources|<span_title="This_citation_requires_a_reference_to_the_specific_page_or_range_of_pages_in_which_the_material_appears. (December_2015)">page needed</span>]]</i>]</sup>-4) Constitutional monarchies differ from [absolute monarchies](/wiki/Absolute_monarchy "Absolute monarchy") (in which a monarch is the only decision-maker) in that they are bound to exercise powers and authorities within limits prescribed by an established legal framework. The monarch is frequently perceived as a visible symbol of [national unity](/wiki/Nationalism "Nationalism").[[5]](#cite_note-r251-5)

The powers of constitutional monarchs vary. In some countries, the monarch has virtually no executive or policy-making power and is primarily a hereditary symbolic head of state (who may be an emperor, king or queen, prince or grand duke),[[6]](#cite_note-g368-6) while in other countries, the monarch has meaningful formal powers (such as [veto power](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent"), appointment power, and power to [dissolve parliament](/wiki/Dissolution_of_parliament "Dissolution of parliament")).[[7]](#cite_note-:1-7)[[8]](#cite_note-e278-8)

In countries such as [Liechtenstein](/wiki/Liechtenstein "Liechtenstein"), [Monaco](/wiki/Monaco "Monaco"), [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco"), [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan"), [Kuwait](/wiki/Kuwait "Kuwait"), [Bahrain](/wiki/Bahrain "Bahrain") and [Bhutan](/wiki/Bhutan "Bhutan"), the constitution grants substantial discretionary powers to the sovereign, while in [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") and other [Commonwealth realms](/wiki/Commonwealth_realm "Commonwealth realm"), the [Netherlands](/wiki/Kingdom_of_the_Netherlands "Kingdom of the Netherlands"), [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"), [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg "Luxembourg"), [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark"), [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway"), [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"), [Lesotho](/wiki/Lesotho "Lesotho"), [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"), [Cambodia](/wiki/Cambodia "Cambodia"), and [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), the monarch retains significantly less power, if any, personal discretion in the exercise of their authority. While many constitutional monarchies are [democratic](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy"), some are not.[[7]](#cite_note-:1-7)

The [Commonwealth realms](/wiki/Commonwealth_realm "Commonwealth realm") share the same person as a ceremonial [hereditary monarchy](/wiki/Hereditary_monarchy "Hereditary monarchy") under the [Westminster system](/wiki/Westminster_system "Westminster system") of constitutional governance.[[9]](#cite_note-k395-9) Two constitutional monarchies – [Malaysia](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Malaysia "Monarchy of Malaysia") and [Cambodia](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Cambodia "Monarchy of Cambodia") – are [elective monarchies](/wiki/Elective_monarchy "Elective monarchy"), in which the ruler is periodically selected by a small [electoral college](/wiki/Electoral_college "Electoral college").[[10]](#cite_note-i689-10)

Some use the term **semi-constitutional monarchy** to identify constitutional monarchies where the monarch retains substantial powers, on a par with a [president](/wiki/President_(government_title) "President (government title)") in a [presidential](/wiki/Presidential_system "Presidential system") or [semi-presidential system](/wiki/Semi-presidential_system "Semi-presidential system").[[11]](#cite_note-11) Numerous liberal democracies restrain monarchic power in practice rather than written law, e.g., the [constitution of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_United_Kingdom "Constitution of the United Kingdom"), which affords the monarch substantial, if limited, legislative and executive powers.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Constitutional_monarchy&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The oldest constitutional monarchy dating back to ancient times was that of the [Hittites](/wiki/Hittites "Hittites"). They were an [ancient Anatolian people](/wiki/Ancient_Anatolian_people "Ancient Anatolian people") that lived during the [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age") whose king had to share his authority with an assembly, called the *[Panku](/wiki/Pankus "Pankus")*, which was the equivalent to a modern-day deliberative assembly or a legislature. Members of the *Panku* came from scattered noble families who worked as representatives of their subjects in an adjutant or subaltern federal-type landscape.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAkurgal2001118-14)

According to [Herodotus](/wiki/Herodotus "Herodotus"), [Demonax](/wiki/Demonax_(lawmaker) "Demonax (lawmaker)") created a constitutional monarchy for King [Battus III](/wiki/Battus_III_of_Cyrene "Battus III of Cyrene") the Lame, of [Cyrene](/wiki/Cyrene,_Libya "Cyrene, Libya"), when [Cyrenaica](/wiki/Cyrenaica "Cyrenaica") had become an unstable state, in about 548 BC.[[15]](#cite_note-Histories-15)

### Constitutional and absolute monarchy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Constitutional_monarchy&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Constitutional and absolute monarchy")]

#### England, Scotland and the United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Constitutional_monarchy&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: England, Scotland and the United Kingdom")]

In the [Kingdom of England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England"), the [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution") of 1688 furthered the constitutional monarchy, restricted by laws such as the [Bill of Rights 1689](/wiki/Bill_of_Rights_1689 "Bill of Rights 1689") and the [Act of Settlement 1701](/wiki/Act_of_Settlement_1701 "Act of Settlement 1701"), although the first form of constitution was enacted with [Magna Carta](/wiki/Magna_Carta "Magna Carta") of 1215. At the same time, in [Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland"), the [Convention of Estates](/wiki/Convention_of_Estates_(1689) "Convention of Estates (1689)") enacted the [Claim of Right Act 1689](/wiki/Claim_of_Right_Act_1689 "Claim of Right Act 1689"), which placed similar limits on the Scottish monarchy.

[Queen Anne](/wiki/Anne,_Queen_of_Great_Britain "Anne, Queen of Great Britain") was the last monarch to veto an Act of Parliament when, on 11 March 1708, she blocked the [Scottish Militia Bill](/wiki/Scottish_Militia_Bill "Scottish Militia Bill"). However Hanoverian monarchs continued to selectively dictate government policies. For instance [King George III](/wiki/King_George_III "King George III") constantly blocked [Catholic Emancipation](/wiki/Catholic_Emancipation_in_Britain "Catholic Emancipation in Britain"), eventually precipitating the resignation of [William Pitt the Younger](/wiki/William_Pitt_the_Younger "William Pitt the Younger") as prime minister in 1801.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The sovereign's influence on the choice of prime minister gradually declined over this period. The [Fox-North Coalition](/wiki/Fox-North_Coalition "Fox-North Coalition") came to power on 2 April 1783, in spite of the King's resistance. It was the first time that [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III") had been allowed no role in determining who should hold government office.[[17]](#cite_note-MI-17)

[King William IV](/wiki/William_IV_of_the_United_Kingdom "William IV of the United Kingdom") was the last monarch to dismiss a prime minister, when in 1834 he removed [Lord Melbourne](/wiki/Lord_Melbourne "Lord Melbourne") as a result of Melbourne's choice of [Lord John Russell](/wiki/Lord_John_Russell,_1st_Earl_Russell "Lord John Russell, 1st Earl Russell") as Leader of the House of Commons.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-Mitchell-19) [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria") was the last monarch to exercise real personal power, but this diminished over the course of her reign. In 1839, she became the last sovereign to keep a prime minister in power against the will of Parliament when the [Bedchamber crisis](/wiki/Bedchamber_crisis "Bedchamber crisis") resulted in the retention of Lord Melbourne's administration.[[20]](#cite_note-20) By the end of her reign, however, she could do nothing to block the unacceptable (to her) premierships of [William Gladstone](/wiki/William_Ewart_Gladstone "William Ewart Gladstone"), although she still exercised power in appointments to the Cabinet. For example, in 1886 she vetoed Gladstone's choice of [Hugh Childers](/wiki/Hugh_Childers "Hugh Childers") as War Secretary in favour of [Sir Henry Campbell-Bannerman](/wiki/Sir_Henry_Campbell-Bannerman "Sir Henry Campbell-Bannerman").[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Today, the role of the British monarch is by convention effectively ceremonial.[[22]](#cite_note-22) The [British Parliament](/wiki/British_Parliament "British Parliament") and the [Government](/wiki/Government_of_the_United_Kingdom "Government of the United Kingdom") – chiefly in the office of [Prime Minister of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Prime Minister of the United Kingdom") – exercise their powers under ["royal (or Crown) prerogative"](/wiki/Royal_prerogative "Royal prerogative"): on behalf of the monarch and through powers still formally possessed by the monarch.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDunt2015-23)[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParliamentary_staff2010-24)

No person may accept significant public office without swearing an [oath of allegiance to the King](/wiki/Oath_of_Allegiance_(United_Kingdom) "Oath of Allegiance (United Kingdom)").[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESear20013-25) With few exceptions, the monarch is bound by [constitutional convention](/wiki/Constitutional_conventions_of_the_United_Kingdom "Constitutional conventions of the United Kingdom") to act on the [advice](/wiki/Advice_(constitutional) "Advice (constitutional)") of the government.

#### Continental Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Constitutional_monarchy&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Continental Europe")]

Poland developed the first constitution for a monarchy in continental Europe, with the [Constitution of 3 May 1791](/wiki/Constitution_of_3_May_1791 "Constitution of 3 May 1791"); it was the second single-document constitution in the world just after the first republican [Constitution of the United States](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_United_States "Constitution of the United States"). Constitutional monarchy also occurred briefly in the early years of the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"), but much more widely afterwards. [Napoleon Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon_Bonaparte "Napoleon Bonaparte") is considered the first monarch proclaiming himself as an embodiment of the nation, rather than as a divinely appointed ruler; this interpretation of monarchy is germane to continental constitutional monarchies. German philosopher [Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel](/wiki/Georg_Wilhelm_Friedrich_Hegel "Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel"), in his work *[Elements of the Philosophy of Right](/wiki/Elements_of_the_Philosophy_of_Right "Elements of the Philosophy of Right")* (1820), gave the concept a philosophical justification that concurred with evolving contemporary political theory and the [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") Christian view of natural law.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHegel1991[[Category:Wikipedia_articles_needing_page_number_citations_from_December_2015]]<sup_class="noprint_Inline-Template_"_style="white-space:nowrap;">[<i>[[Wikipedia:Citing_sources|<span_title="This_citation_requires_a_reference_to_the_specific_page_or_range_of_pages_in_which_the_material_appears. (December_2015)">page needed</span>]]</i>]</sup>-26) Hegel's forecast of a constitutional monarch with very limited powers whose function is to embody the national character and provide constitutional continuity in times of emergency was reflected in the development of constitutional monarchies in Europe and Japan.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHegel1991[[Category:Wikipedia_articles_needing_page_number_citations_from_December_2015]]<sup_class="noprint_Inline-Template_"_style="white-space:nowrap;">[<i>[[Wikipedia:Citing_sources|<span_title="This_citation_requires_a_reference_to_the_specific_page_or_range_of_pages_in_which_the_material_appears. (December_2015)">page needed</span>]]</i>]</sup>-26)

#### Executive monarchy versus ceremonial monarchy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Constitutional_monarchy&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Executive monarchy versus ceremonial monarchy")]

There exist at least two different types of constitutional monarchies in the modern world – executive and ceremonial.[[27]](#cite_note-:0-27) In executive monarchies (also called *semi-constitutional monarchies*), the monarch wields significant (though not [absolute](/wiki/Absolute_monarchy "Absolute monarchy")) power. The monarchy under this system of government is a powerful political (and social) institution. *Semi-monarchy* is a distinct regime type characterized by a [collegial executive](/wiki/Collegial_executive "Collegial executive"), a hereditary monarch with substantive powers who appoints the cabinet, dual cabinet accountability to both the monarch and the legislature, and the monarch's authority to dissolve the assembly.[[28]](#cite_note-28) In [*Federalist No. 69*](/wiki/Federalist_No._69 "Federalist No. 69"), written in 1788, [Alexander Hamilton](/wiki/Alexander_Hamilton "Alexander Hamilton") described the powers of the [President of the United States](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States").

By contrast, in ceremonial monarchies, the monarch holds little or no actual power or direct political influence, though they frequently still have a great deal of social and cultural influence. Ceremonial and executive monarchy should not be confused with democratic and non-democratic monarchical systems. For example, in Liechtenstein and Monaco, the ruling monarchs wield significant executive power. However, while they are theoretically very powerful within their small states, they are *not* absolute monarchs and have very limited *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* power compared to the [Islamic monarchs](/wiki/Monarchy#Monarchies_in_the_Muslim_world "Monarchy"), which is why their countries are generally considered to be [liberal democracies](/wiki/Liberal_democracies "Liberal democracies") and not undemocratic.[[27]](#cite_note-:0-27)

For instance, when [Hereditary Prince Alois](/wiki/Hereditary_Prince_Alois "Hereditary Prince Alois") of Liechtenstein threatened to veto a possible approval of a [referendum to legalize abortion in 2011](/wiki/2011_Liechtenstein_referendums "2011 Liechtenstein referendums"), it came as a surprise because the prince had not vetoed any law for over 30 years[[29]](#cite_note-29) (in the end, this was moot, as the proposal was not approved).

### Modern constitutional monarchy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Constitutional_monarchy&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Modern constitutional monarchy")]

As originally conceived, a constitutional monarch was head of the [executive branch](/wiki/Executive_branch "Executive branch") and quite a powerful figure even though their power was limited by the constitution and the elected parliament. Some of the framers of the U.S. Constitution may have envisioned the president as an elected constitutional monarch, as the term was then understood, following [Montesquieu's](/wiki/Baron_de_Montesquieu "Baron de Montesquieu") account of the separation of powers.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaron_de_Montesquieu1924[[Category:Wikipedia_articles_needing_page_number_citations_from_December_2015]]<sup_class="noprint_Inline-Template_"_style="white-space:nowrap;">[<i>[[Wikipedia:Citing_sources|<span_title="This_citation_requires_a_reference_to_the_specific_page_or_range_of_pages_in_which_the_material_appears. (December_2015)">page needed</span>]]</i>]</sup>-30)

The present-day concept of a constitutional monarchy developed primarily in the United Kingdom, where a democratically elected parliament and the executive government formed from the ranks of the majority party, led by a [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_minister "Prime minister"), exercise true power while the monarch remains as a largely vestigial and constrained position (in the Commonwealth nations, the monarch is bound to take the advice of his or her government ministers, and has very limited 'reserve powers').

Nations of the Commonwealth and European democracies are where much of the working out of the contours and limits of constitutional monarchs' powers has been done, primarily over the last century. In the course of [France](/wiki/France "France")'s [July Monarchy](/wiki/July_Monarchy "July Monarchy"), [Louis-Philippe I](/wiki/Louis-Philippe_I "Louis-Philippe I") was styled "King of the French" rather than "King of France". Following the [unification of Germany](/wiki/Unification_of_Germany "Unification of Germany"), [Otto von Bismarck](/wiki/Otto_von_Bismarck "Otto von Bismarck") rejected the British model. In the constitutional monarchy established under the [Constitution of the German Empire](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_German_Empire "Constitution of the German Empire") which Bismarck inspired, the [Kaiser](/wiki/Kaiser "Kaiser") retained considerable actual executive power, while the [Imperial Chancellor](/wiki/Chancellor_of_Germany#Empire_(1871–1918) "Chancellor of Germany") needed no parliamentary vote of confidence and ruled solely by the imperial mandate. However, this model of constitutional monarchy was discredited and abolished following Germany's defeat in the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War"). Later, [Fascist Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy_under_Fascism_(1922%E2%80%931943) "Kingdom of Italy under Fascism (1922–1943)") could also be considered a constitutional monarchy, in that there was a [king](/wiki/King_of_Italy "King of Italy") as the titular head of state while actual power was held by [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini") under a constitution. This eventually discredited the Italian monarchy and led to its abolition in 1946. After the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), surviving European monarchies almost invariably adopted some variant of the constitutional monarchy model originally developed in Britain.

A [parliamentary democracy](/wiki/Parliamentary_democracy "Parliamentary democracy") may be a constitutional monarchy or a [republic](/wiki/Republic "Republic"), differing only in terms of titles and rules of succession rather than in substantial exercise of power. In both cases, the titular head of state – monarch or president – serves the traditional role of embodying and representing the nation, while the government is carried on by a cabinet composed predominantly of elected [Members of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament "Member of Parliament").

However, three important factors distinguish monarchies such as the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") from systems where greater power might otherwise rest with [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament "Parliament"). These are:

* The [royal prerogative](/wiki/Royal_prerogative "Royal prerogative"), under which the monarch may exercise power under certain very limited circumstances
* [Sovereign immunity](/wiki/Sovereign_immunity "Sovereign immunity"), under which the monarch may *do no wrong* under the law because the [responsible government](/wiki/Responsible_government "Responsible government") is instead deemed accountable
* The immunity of the monarch from some taxation or restrictions on property use

Other privileges may be nominal or ceremonial (e.g., where the executive, judiciary, police or armed forces act on the authority of or owe [allegiance](/wiki/Allegiance "Allegiance") to the Crown).

Today slightly more than a quarter of constitutional monarchies are [Western European](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe") countries, including the [United Kingdom](/wiki/Monarchy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Monarchy of the United Kingdom"), [Spain](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Spain "Monarchy of Spain"), the [Netherlands](/wiki/Monarchy_of_the_Netherlands "Monarchy of the Netherlands"), [Belgium](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Belgium "Monarchy of Belgium"), [Norway](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Norway "Monarchy of Norway"), [Denmark](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Denmark "Monarchy of Denmark"), [Luxembourg](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Luxembourg "Monarchy of Luxembourg"), [Monaco](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Monaco "Monarchy of Monaco"), [Liechtenstein](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Liechtenstein "Monarchy of Liechtenstein") and [Sweden](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Sweden "Monarchy of Sweden"). However, the two most populous constitutional monarchies in the world are in Asia: [Japan](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Japan "Monarchy of Japan") and [Thailand](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Thailand "Monarchy of Thailand"). In these countries, the [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_minister "Prime minister") holds the day-to-day powers of governance, while the monarch retains residual (but not always insignificant) powers. The powers of the monarch differ between countries. In Denmark and in Belgium, for example, the monarch formally appoints a representative to preside over the creation of a [coalition government](/wiki/Coalition_government "Coalition government") following a parliamentary election, while in Norway the King chairs special meetings of the [cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_(government) "Cabinet (government)").

In nearly all cases, the monarch is still the nominal chief executive, but is bound by convention to act on the advice of the Cabinet. However, a few monarchies (most notably [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") and [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden")) have amended their constitutions so that the monarch is no longer the nominal chief executive.

There are fifteen constitutional monarchies under King [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III"), which are known as [Commonwealth realms](/wiki/Commonwealth_realm "Commonwealth realm").[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoyal_Household_staff2015b-31) Unlike some of their continental European counterparts, the Monarch and his Governors-General in the Commonwealth realms hold significant "reserve" or "prerogative" powers, to be wielded in times of extreme emergency or constitutional crises, usually to uphold parliamentary government. For example, during the [1975 Australian constitutional crisis](/wiki/1975_Australian_constitutional_crisis "1975 Australian constitutional crisis"), the Governor-General dismissed the Australian Prime Minister [Gough Whitlam](/wiki/Gough_Whitlam "Gough Whitlam"). The [Australian Senate](/wiki/Australian_Senate "Australian Senate") had threatened to block the Government's [budget](/wiki/Budget "Budget") by refusing to pass the necessary appropriation bills. On 11 November 1975, Whitlam intended to call a half-Senate election to try to break the deadlock. When he sought the Governor-General's approval of the election, the Governor-General instead dismissed him as prime minister. Shortly after that, he installed leader of the opposition [Malcolm Fraser](/wiki/Malcolm_Fraser "Malcolm Fraser") in his place. Acting quickly before all parliamentarians became aware of the government change, Fraser and his allies secured passage of the appropriation bills, and the Governor-General dissolved Parliament for a [double dissolution](/wiki/Double_dissolution "Double dissolution") election. Fraser and his government were returned with a massive majority. This led to much speculation among Whitlam's supporters as to whether this use of the Governor-General's reserve powers was appropriate, and whether [Australia should become a republic](/wiki/Republicanism_in_Australia "Republicanism in Australia"). Among supporters of constitutional monarchy, however, the event confirmed the monarchy's value as a source of checks and balances against elected politicians who might seek powers in excess of those conferred by the constitution, and ultimately as a safeguard against dictatorship.

In Thailand's constitutional monarchy, the monarch is recognized as the Head of State, Head of the Armed Forces, Upholder of the Buddhist Religion, and Defender of the Faith. The immediate former King, [Bhumibol Adulyadej](/wiki/Bhumibol_Adulyadej "Bhumibol Adulyadej"), was among the longest-reigning monarch in the world and the longest in all of Thailand's history, before dying on 13 October 2016.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Bhumibol reigned through several political changes in the Thai government. He played an influential role in each incident, often acting as mediator between disputing political opponents. (See Bhumibol's role in [Thai Politics](/wiki/Bhumibol_Adulyadej#Role_in_Thai_politics "Bhumibol Adulyadej").) Among the powers retained by the Thai monarch under the constitution, [lèse majesté](/wiki/L%C3%A8se_majest%C3%A9_in_Thailand "Lèse majesté in Thailand") protects the image of the monarch and enables him to play a role in politics. It carries strict criminal penalties for violators. Generally, the Thai people were reverent of Bhumibol. Much of his social influence arose from this reverence and from the socioeconomic improvement efforts undertaken by the royal family.

In the United Kingdom, a frequent debate centres on when it is appropriate for a British monarch to act. When a monarch does act, political controversy can often ensue, partially because the neutrality of the crown is seen to be compromised in favour of a [partisan](/wiki/Partisan_(political) "Partisan (political)") goal, while some [political scientists](/wiki/Political_scientist "Political scientist") champion the idea of an "interventionist monarch" as a check against possible illegal action by politicians. For instance, the monarch of the United Kingdom can theoretically exercise an absolute veto over legislation by withholding royal assent. However, no monarch has done so since 1708, and it is widely believed that this and many of the monarch's other political powers are [lapsed powers](/wiki/Lapsed_power "Lapsed power").

In *[The English Constitution](/wiki/The_English_Constitution "The English Constitution")*, British political theorist [Walter Bagehot](/wiki/Walter_Bagehot "Walter Bagehot") identified three main political rights which a constitutional monarch may freely exercise: the right to be consulted, the right to encourage, and the right to warn. Many constitutional monarchies still retain significant authorities or political influence, however, such as through certain [reserve powers](/wiki/Reserve_power "Reserve power"), and may also play an important political role. Political scientist [Vernon Bogdanor](/wiki/Vernon_Bogdanor "Vernon Bogdanor"), paraphrasing [Thomas Macaulay](/wiki/Thomas_Macaulay "Thomas Macaulay"), has defined a constitutional monarch as "A sovereign who reigns but does not rule".[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBogdanor1996407–422-33) Strongly limited constitutional monarchies, such as those of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/Monarchy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Monarchy of the United Kingdom") and [Australia](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Australia "Monarchy of Australia"), have been referred to as [crowned republics](/wiki/Crowned_republic "Crowned republic") by writers [H. G. Wells](/wiki/H._G._Wells "H. G. Wells") and Glenn Patmore.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)

1. **[^](#cite_ref-UAE_40-0)** In the case of the [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates "United Arab Emirates"), the [president](/wiki/President_of_the_United_Arab_Emirates "President of the United Arab Emirates") functions as the head of state of a federation of seven [absolute monarchies](/wiki/Absolute_monarchy "Absolute monarchy"), and is *[de jure](/wiki/De_jure "De jure")* elected by the [Federal Supreme Council](/wiki/Federal_Supreme_Council "Federal Supreme Council") from among its members; the [prime minister](/wiki/List_of_prime_ministers_of_the_United_Arab_Emirates "List of prime ministers of the United Arab Emirates") is *de jure* appointed and is the head of government. The president and prime minister are *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* the rulers of the absolute monarchies of [Abu Dhabi](/wiki/Emirate_of_Abu_Dhabi "Emirate of Abu Dhabi") and [Dubai](/wiki/Emirate_of_Dubai "Emirate of Dubai"), respectively.

## Former constitutional monarchies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Constitutional_monarchy&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Former constitutional monarchies")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |

* The [Kingdom of Afghanistan](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Afghanistan "Kingdom of Afghanistan") was a constitutional monarchy under [Mohammad Zahir Shah](/wiki/Mohammad_Zahir_Shah "Mohammad Zahir Shah") from 1964 to 1973.[[37]](#cite_note-41)
* The [Hashemite Kingdom of Iraq](/wiki/Hashemite_Kingdom_of_Iraq "Hashemite Kingdom of Iraq") was a constitutional monarchy until 1958 when [King Faisal II](/wiki/King_Faisal_II "King Faisal II") was deposed in a military coup.[[38]](#cite_note-42)
* The [Anglo-Corsican Kingdom](/wiki/Anglo-Corsican_Kingdom "Anglo-Corsican Kingdom") was a brief period in the [history of Corsica](/wiki/History_of_Corsica "History of Corsica") (1794–1796) when the island broke with [Revolutionary France](/wiki/Revolutionary_France "Revolutionary France") and sought military protection from [Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain"). Corsica became an independent kingdom under [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III") of the United Kingdom, but with its own elected parliament and a written constitution guaranteeing local autonomy and democratic rights.
* [Barbados](/wiki/Barbados "Barbados") from gaining its independence in 1966 until 2021, was a constitutional monarchy in the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") with a [Governor-General](/wiki/Governor-General_of_Barbados "Governor-General of Barbados") representing the [Monarchy of Barbados](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Barbados "Monarchy of Barbados"). After an extensive history of [republican](/wiki/Republicanism_in_Barbados "Republicanism in Barbados") movements, a republic was declared on 30 November 2021.
* [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") from 1822, with the proclamation of independence and rise of the [Empire of Brazil](/wiki/Empire_of_Brazil "Empire of Brazil") by [Pedro I of Brazil](/wiki/Pedro_I_of_Brazil "Pedro I of Brazil") to 1889, when [Pedro II](/wiki/Pedro_II_of_Brazil "Pedro II of Brazil") was deposed by a military coup.
* [Tsardom of Bulgaria](/wiki/Third_Bulgarian_Empire "Third Bulgarian Empire") until 1946 when [Tsar Simeon](/wiki/Simeon_Saxe-Coburg-Gotha "Simeon Saxe-Coburg-Gotha") was deposed by the communist assembly.
* Many [republics in the Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Republics_in_the_Commonwealth_of_Nations "Republics in the Commonwealth of Nations") were constitutional monarchies for some period after their independence, including [South Africa](/wiki/Union_of_South_Africa "Union of South Africa") (1910–1961), [Ceylon](/wiki/Dominion_of_Ceylon "Dominion of Ceylon") (now [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka")) (1948–1972), [Fiji](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Fiji "Monarchy of Fiji") (1970–1987), [Gambia](/wiki/Queen_of_the_Gambia "Queen of the Gambia") (1965–1970), [Ghana](/wiki/Queen_of_Ghana "Queen of Ghana") (1957–1960), [Guyana](/wiki/Queen_of_Guyana "Queen of Guyana") (1966–1970), [Mauritius](/wiki/Queen_of_Mauritius "Queen of Mauritius") (1968–1992), [Trinidad and Tobago](/wiki/Queen_of_Trinidad_and_Tobago "Queen of Trinidad and Tobago") (1962–1976), and [Barbados](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Barbados "Monarchy of Barbados") (1966–2021).
* [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") was a constitutional monarchy starting from the later part of the [Khedivate](/wiki/Khedivate_of_Egypt "Khedivate of Egypt"), with parliamentary structures and a responsible khedival ministry developing in the 1860s and 1870s. The constitutional system continued through the Khedivate period and developed during the [Sultanate](/wiki/Sultanate_of_Egypt "Sultanate of Egypt") and then [Kingdom of Egypt](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Egypt "Kingdom of Egypt"), which established an essentially democratic liberal constitutional regime under the [Egyptian Constitution of 1923](/wiki/Egyptian_Constitution_of_1923 "Egyptian Constitution of 1923"). This system persisted until the declaration of [a republic](/wiki/History_of_Egypt_under_Gamal_Abdel_Nasser#Republic_of_Egypt_(1953–1958) "History of Egypt under Gamal Abdel Nasser") after the [Free Officers Movement](/wiki/Free_Officers_Movement_(Egypt) "Free Officers Movement (Egypt)") coup in 1952. For most of this period, however, Egypt was [occupied by the United Kingdom](/wiki/History_of_Egypt_under_the_British "History of Egypt under the British"), and overall political control was in the hands of British colonial officials [nominally accredited as diplomats](/wiki/List_of_ambassadors_of_the_United_Kingdom_to_Egypt "List of ambassadors of the United Kingdom to Egypt") to the Egyptian royal court but actually able to overrule any decision of the monarch or elected government.
* The [Grand Duchy of Finland](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Finland "Grand Duchy of Finland") was a constitutional monarchy though its ruler, [Alexander I](/wiki/Alexander_I_of_Russia "Alexander I of Russia"), was simultaneously an [autocrat](/wiki/Autocracy "Autocracy") and [absolute ruler](/wiki/Absolute_monarchy "Absolute monarchy") in Russia.
* [France](/wiki/France "France"), several times from 1789 through the 19th century. The transformation of the [Estates General of 1789](/wiki/Estates_General_of_1789 "Estates General of 1789") into the [National Assembly](/wiki/National_Constituent_Assembly_(France) "National Constituent Assembly (France)") initiated an ad-hoc transition from the absolute monarchy of the *[Ancien Régime](/wiki/Ancien_R%C3%A9gime "Ancien Régime")* to a new constitutional system. France formally became an executive constitutional monarchy with the promulgation of the [French Constitution of 1791](/wiki/French_Constitution_of_1791 "French Constitution of 1791"), which took effect on 1 October of that year. This first French constitutional monarchy was short-lived, ending with the overthrow of the monarchy and establishment of the [French First Republic](/wiki/French_First_Republic "French First Republic") after the [Insurrection of 10 August 1792](/wiki/Insurrection_of_10_August_1792 "Insurrection of 10 August 1792"). Several years later, in 1804, [Napoleon Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon_Bonaparte "Napoleon Bonaparte") proclaimed himself Emperor of the French in what was ostensibly a constitutional monarchy, though modern historians often call his reign as an absolute monarchy.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The [Bourbon Restoration](/wiki/Bourbon_Restoration_in_France "Bourbon Restoration in France") (under [Louis XVIII](/wiki/Louis_XVIII "Louis XVIII") and [Charles X](/wiki/Charles_X_of_France "Charles X of France")), the [July Monarchy](/wiki/July_Monarchy "July Monarchy") (under [Louis-Philippe](/wiki/Louis-Philippe "Louis-Philippe")), and the [Second Empire](/wiki/Second_French_Empire "Second French Empire") (under [Napoleon III](/wiki/Napoleon_III "Napoleon III")) were also constitutional monarchies, although the power of the monarch varied considerably between them and sometimes within them.
* The [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") from 1871 to 1918, (as well as earlier confederations, and the monarchies it consisted of) was also a constitutional monarchy—see [Constitution of the German Empire](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_German_Empire "Constitution of the German Empire").
* [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece") until 1973 when [Constantine II](/wiki/Constantine_II_of_Greece "Constantine II of Greece") was deposed by the [military government](/wiki/Greek_military_junta_of_1967%E2%80%9374 "Greek military junta of 1967–74"). The decision was formalized by a [plebiscite](/wiki/Greek_plebiscite,_1974 "Greek plebiscite, 1974") 8 December 1974.
* [Hawaii](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hawaii "Kingdom of Hawaii"), which was an absolute monarchy from its founding in 1810, transitioned to a constitutional monarchy in 1840 when [King Kamehameha III](/wiki/King_Kamehameha_III "King Kamehameha III") promulgated the kingdom's [first constitution](/wiki/1840_Constitution_of_the_Kingdom_of_Hawaii "1840 Constitution of the Kingdom of Hawaii"). This constitutional form of government continued until the monarchy was overthrown in an 1893 [coup](/wiki/Overthrow_of_the_Kingdom_of_Hawaii#Coup_d.27état "Overthrow of the Kingdom of Hawaii").
* The [Kingdom of Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary "Kingdom of Hungary"). In 1848–1849 and 1867–1918 as part of [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary"). In the interwar period (1920–1944) [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary") remained a constitutional monarchy without a reigning monarch.
* [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland"). The Act of Union, a 1 December 1918 agreement with Denmark, established Iceland as a [sovereign kingdom](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Iceland "Monarchy of Iceland") united with Denmark under a common king. Iceland abolished the monarchy and became a republic on 17 June 1944 after the Icelandic constitutional referendum, 24 May 1944.
* [India](/wiki/Dominion_of_India "Dominion of India") was a constitutional monarchy, with [George VI](/wiki/George_VI "George VI") as head of state and [the Earl Mountbatten](/wiki/Louis_Mountbatten,_1st_Earl_Mountbatten_of_Burma "Louis Mountbatten, 1st Earl Mountbatten of Burma") as [governor-general](/wiki/Governor-General_of_India "Governor-General of India"), for a brief period between gaining its independence from the British on [15 August 1947](/wiki/Independence_Day_(India) "Independence Day (India)") and becoming a republic when it adopted [its constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_India "Constitution of India") on 26 January 1950, henceforth celebrated as [Republic Day](/wiki/Republic_Day_(India) "Republic Day (India)").
* [Iran](/wiki/Pahlavi_Iran "Pahlavi Iran") under [Mohammad Reza Pahlavi](/wiki/Mohammad_Reza_Pahlavi "Mohammad Reza Pahlavi") was a semi-constitutional monarchy, which had been originally established during the [Persian Constitutional Revolution](/wiki/Persian_Constitutional_Revolution "Persian Constitutional Revolution") in 1906.
* [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy") until 2 June 1946, when a referendum proclaimed the end of the Kingdom and the beginning of the Republic.
* The [Kingdom of Laos](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Laos "Kingdom of Laos") was a constitutional monarchy until 1975, when [Sisavang Vatthana](/wiki/Savang_Vatthana "Savang Vatthana") was forced to abdicate by the communist [Pathet Lao](/wiki/Pathet_Lao "Pathet Lao").
* [Malta](/wiki/Malta "Malta") was a constitutional monarchy with [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") as Queen of Malta, represented by a Governor-General appointed by her, for the first ten years of independence from 21 September 1964 to the declaration of the Republic of Malta on 13 December 1974.
* [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico") was twice an Empire. The [First Mexican Empire](/wiki/First_Mexican_Empire "First Mexican Empire") lasted from 19 May 1822 to 19 March 1823, with [Agustin I](/wiki/Agust%C3%ADn_de_Iturbide "Agustín de Iturbide") elected as emperor. Then, the [Mexican monarchists](/wiki/Monarchism_in_Mexico "Monarchism in Mexico") and conservatives, with the help of the Austrian and Spanish crowns and [Napoleon III of France](/wiki/Napoleon_III_of_France "Napoleon III of France"), elected [Maximilian of Austria](/wiki/Maximilian_of_Mexico "Maximilian of Mexico") as [Emperor of Mexico](/wiki/Emperor_of_Mexico "Emperor of Mexico"). This constitutional monarchy lasted three years, from 1864 to 1867.
* [Montenegro](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Montenegro "Kingdom of Montenegro") until 1918 when it merged with [Serbia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Serbia "Kingdom of Serbia") and other areas to form [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Yugoslavia "Kingdom of Yugoslavia").
* [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal") until 28 May 2008, when [King Gyanendra](/wiki/King_Gyanendra "King Gyanendra") was deposed, and the Federal Democratic Republic of Nepal was declared.
* Nigeria was a constitutional monarchy from its independence in 1960 till it became a Republic in 1963. Its only monarch was Queen Elizabeth II, Queen of Nigeria.
* [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") from 1876 until 1878 and again from 1908 until the dissolution of the empire in 1922.
* [Pakistan](/wiki/Dominion_of_Pakistan "Dominion of Pakistan") was a constitutional monarchy for a brief period between gaining its independence from the British on [14 August 1947](/wiki/Independence_Day_(Pakistan) "Independence Day (Pakistan)") and becoming a republic when it adopted the [first Constitution of Pakistan](/wiki/Constitution_of_Pakistan_of_1956 "Constitution of Pakistan of 1956") on 23 March 1956. The [Dominion of Pakistan](/wiki/Dominion_of_Pakistan "Dominion of Pakistan") had a total of two monarchs ([George VI](/wiki/George_VI "George VI") and [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II")) and four [Governors-General](/wiki/Governor-General_of_Pakistan "Governor-General of Pakistan") ([Muhammad Ali Jinnah](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali_Jinnah "Muhammad Ali Jinnah") being the first). [Republic Day](/wiki/Republic_Day_(Pakistan) "Republic Day (Pakistan)") (or Pakistan Day) is celebrated every year on 23 March to commemorate the adoption of its Constitution and the transition of the Dominion of Pakistan to the [Islamic Republic of Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan").
* The [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth"), formed after the [Union of Lublin](/wiki/Union_of_Lublin "Union of Lublin") in 1569 and lasting until the final partition of the state in 1795, operated much like many modern European constitutional monarchies (into which it was officially changed by the establishment of the [Constitution of 3 May 1791](/wiki/Constitution_of_3_May_1791 "Constitution of 3 May 1791"), which historian [Norman Davies](/wiki/Norman_Davies "Norman Davies") calls "the first constitution of its kind in Europe").[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavies1996699-43) The legislators of the unified state truly did not see it as a monarchy at all, but as *a republic under the presidency of the King* . Poland–Lithuania also followed the principle of *Rex regnat et non gubernat*, had a bicameral parliament, and a collection of entrenched legal documents amounting to a constitution along the lines of the modern [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"). The King was elected and had the duty of maintaining the people's rights.
* [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal") was a monarchy since 1139 and a constitutional monarchy from 1822 to 1828, and again from 1834 until 1910, when [Manuel II](/wiki/Manuel_II_of_Portugal "Manuel II of Portugal") was overthrown by a military coup. From 1815 to 1825 it was part of the [United Kingdom of Portugal, Brazil and the Algarves](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Portugal,_Brazil_and_the_Algarves "United Kingdom of Portugal, Brazil and the Algarves") which was a constitutional monarchy for the years 1820–23.
* [Kingdom of Romania](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Romania "Kingdom of Romania") from its establishment in 1881 until 1947 when [Michael I](/wiki/Michael_I_of_Romania "Michael I of Romania") was forced to abdicate by the communists.
* [Kingdom of Serbia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Serbia "Kingdom of Serbia") from 1882 until 1918, when it merged with the State of Slovenes, Croats and Serbs into the unitary Yugoslav Kingdom, that was led by the Serbian [Karadjordjevic](/wiki/Karadjordjevic "Karadjordjevic") dynasty.
* [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"), then [part of Japan](/wiki/Taiwan_under_Japanese_rule "Taiwan under Japanese rule") from 1895 to 1945, was a constitutional monarchy with the [Emperor of Japan](/wiki/Emperor_of_Japan "Emperor of Japan"), who was the head of state, with the [Governor-General](/wiki/Governor-General_of_Taiwan "Governor-General of Taiwan") served as the head of government.
* [Trinidad and Tobago](/wiki/Trinidad_and_Tobago "Trinidad and Tobago") was a constitutional monarchy with [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") as [Queen of Trinidad and Tobago](/wiki/Queen_of_Trinidad_and_Tobago "Queen of Trinidad and Tobago"), represented by a [Governor-General](/wiki/Governor-General_of_Trinidad_and_Tobago "Governor-General of Trinidad and Tobago") appointed by her, for the first fourteen years of independence from 31 August 1962 to the declaration of the Republic of Trinidad and Tobago on 1 August 1976. Republic Day is celebrated every year on 24 September.
* [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Yugoslavia "Kingdom of Yugoslavia") from 1918 (as [Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Serbs,_Croats_and_Slovenes "Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes")) until 1929 and from 1931 (as [Kingdom of Yugoslavia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Yugoslavia "Kingdom of Yugoslavia")) until 1945 when the communist government abolished the monarchy under [Peter II](/wiki/Peter_II_of_Yugoslavia "Peter II of Yugoslavia").
* [Vietnam](/wiki/State_of_Vietnam "State of Vietnam") was a semi-constitutional monarchy from 1949 to 1955, [Bảo Đại](/wiki/B%E1%BA%A3o_%C4%90%E1%BA%A1i "Bảo Đại") was its emperor but he was called the Head of State (*Quốc trưởng*), political power was also in the hands of the government and the National Advisory Council. Vietnam under Bảo Đại planned to organize parliamentary elections and promulgate a constitution, but not in time due to the progress of the [war](/wiki/First_Indochina_War "First Indochina War").

* [Andorra](/wiki/Andorra "Andorra") is a [diarchy](/wiki/Diarchy "Diarchy"), being headed by [two co-princes](/wiki/Co-princes_of_Andorra "Co-princes of Andorra"): the [bishop of Urgell](/wiki/Bishop_of_Urgell "Bishop of Urgell") and the [president of France](/wiki/President_of_France "President of France").
* [Andorra](/wiki/Andorra "Andorra"), [Monaco](/wiki/Monaco "Monaco") and [Liechtenstein](/wiki/Liechtenstein "Liechtenstein") are the only countries with reigning princes.
* [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium") is the only remaining explicit [popular monarchy](/wiki/Popular_monarchy "Popular monarchy"): the formal title of its [king](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Belgium "Monarchy of Belgium") is *King of the Belgians* rather than *King of Belgium*. Historically, several defunct constitutional monarchies followed this model; the Belgian formulation is recognized to have been modelled on the title "King of the French" granted by the [Charter of 1830](/wiki/Charter_of_1830 "Charter of 1830") to monarch of the [July Monarchy](/wiki/July_Monarchy "July Monarchy").
* [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") is the only country remaining with an [emperor](/wiki/Emperor_of_Japan "Emperor of Japan").[[40]](#cite_note-44)
* [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg "Luxembourg") is the only country remaining with a [grand duke](/wiki/Grand_duke "Grand duke").
* [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia") is a [federal](/wiki/Federation "Federation") country with an [elective monarchy](/wiki/Elective_monarchy "Elective monarchy"): the [King of Malaysia](/wiki/King_of_Malaysia "King of Malaysia") (Yang di-Pertuan Agong) is selected from among nine [state](/wiki/States_and_federal_territories_of_Malaysia "States and federal territories of Malaysia") rulers who are also constitutional monarchs themselves.
* [Papua New Guinea](/wiki/Papua_New_Guinea "Papua New Guinea"). Unlike in most other Commonwealth realms, sovereignty is constitutionally vested in the citizenry of Papua New Guinea and the preamble to the constitution states "that all power belongs to the people—acting through their duly elected representatives". The monarch has been, according to section 82 of the constitution, "requested by the people of Papua New Guinea, through their Constituent Assembly, to become [monarch] and Head of State of Papua New Guinea" and thus acts in that capacity.
* [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"). [The Spanish Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Spain "Constitution of Spain") does not recognize the [Spanish Monarch](/wiki/King_of_Spain "King of Spain") as the *sovereign*, but as the [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") [Section 56(1)]. It states "National Sovereignty belongs to the [Spanish People](/wiki/Spanish_people "Spanish people"), from whom all state powers emanate." [Section 1(2)]. [Spanish Constitution | Spanish Senate](https://www.senado.es/web/conocersenado/normas/constitucion/detalleconstitucioncompleta/index.html?lang=en#t2)
* [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates "United Arab Emirates") is a [federal](/wiki/Federation "Federation") country with an [elective monarchy](/wiki/Elective_monarchy "Elective monarchy"), the [President or Ra'is](/wiki/President_of_the_United_Arab_Emirates "President of the United Arab Emirates"), being selected from among the rulers of the seven [emirates](/wiki/Emirates_of_the_United_Arab_Emirates "Emirates of the United Arab Emirates"), each of whom is a hereditary absolute monarch in their own emirate.
